question: Based on Figure 1.2-1 (site layout) and Figure 5.4-7 (DHRS), is the reactor pool arrangement adequate for multi-module DHRS operation?

nuscale_ch01_0069.title: Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams
nuscale_ch01_0069.summary: This section describes the use of simplified electrical, instrumentation and control (I&C), and mechanical drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information. It references tables and figures that list and explain the symbols used in these diagrams, and notes that site-specific diagrams and legends will be provided by applicants referencing the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch01_0068.title: Electrical and Instrumentation and Control Drawings
nuscale_ch01_0068.summary: This section explains that the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report includes simplified electrical, instrumentation, and control (I&C) drawings to visually clarify design information alongside the written text. It references tables and figures listing and explaining the symbols used in these diagrams, and notes that applicants referencing the NuScale design must provide additional site-specific diagrams and legends as needed.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat following reactor shutdown, especially when normal cooling is unavailable, ensuring safe shutdown without external power. It maintains the reactor coolant system (RCS) temperature below 420°F within 36 hours to protect fuel and system integrity, and its reliability and risk significance are evaluated through established programs. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), but both can function together if needed.

nuscale_ch01_0016.title: Principal Site Characteristics
nuscale_ch01_0016.summary: This section provides a general overview of the NuScale plant design, outlining key systems such as safety features, power conversion, fuel handling, and waste management. It explains that while the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) serves as a reference for applicants, certain site-specific and conceptual design information must be provided separately, with clear distinctions made between certified and site-specific design elements. Security-sensitive details are specially marked and withheld according to regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0015.title: General Plant Description
nuscale_ch01_0015.summary: This section provides a summary of the NuScale plant’s overall design, including key systems such as safety features, power conversion, fuel handling, cooling water, and waste management. It explains the distinction between certified and site-specific design elements, clarifies the use of representative information for context, and notes how security-related details are handled. Each COL applicant must reference the NuScale FSAR and provide site-specific information as required.

nuscale_ch01_0043.title: Ultimate Heat Sink
nuscale_ch01_0043.summary: The Ultimate Heat Sink section describes a large, stainless steel-lined, reinforced concrete pool that serves as the final heat removal system for the NuScale plant. In normal operations, heat is removed via a cooling system and rejected to the atmosphere, while in the event of a total loss of AC power, decay heat is passively transferred to the pool, which has sufficient water inventory to ensure continued cooling through natural circulation and boiling.

nuscale_ch05_0023.title: Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents
nuscale_ch05_0023.summary: This section describes the testing and inspection requirements for the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) components, including compliance with ASME codes for preservice and inservice inspections. It details how DHRS actuation valves are exercised during cold shutdown conditions, outlines the required testing procedures for valve operation and position verification, and specifies that a one-time in-situ DHRS heat removal test will be conducted on the first reactor module during initial startup.

nuscale_ch01_0009.title: Regulatory Guide 1.206
nuscale_ch01_0009.summary: The NuScale containment vessel is a cylindrical, steel, ASME-certified pressure vessel designed to withstand high-pressure events and operates under vacuum during normal conditions. The NuScale reactor is a passive, integral small modular pressurized water reactor (SMR) with natural circulation and no need for reactor coolant pumps, and each module produces 160 MWt (about 50 MWe), with a 12-module plant yielding a net output of approximately 570 MWe. Applicants referencing the NuScale design must provide construction and operational schedules.

nuscale_ch01_0034.title: Reactor Building
nuscale_ch01_0034.summary: The Reactor Building (RXB) is centrally located on the NuScale Power Plant site and houses the NuScale Power Modules (NPMs) along with essential systems for plant operation and shutdown. It is a reinforced concrete, Seismic Category I structure designed for safety against various hazards, and provides radiation protection for personnel. The RXB contains a common reactor pool with twelve NPM bays arranged in two rows along the pool’s north and south walls.

nuscale_ch01_0003.title: Plant Location
nuscale_ch01_0003.summary: The section introduces the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), which is required for standard design certification under 10 CFR 52. It describes the modular, scalable design of the NuScale Power Module (NPM), highlighting its key safety features, compact structure, and the ability to operate between one and twelve modules within a single facility. The report covers both individual module design and multi-module plant interfaces, emphasizing safety, efficiency, and advanced digital controls.

nuscale_ch01_0032.title: Radioactive Waste Management System
nuscale_ch01_0032.summary: The section describes the radioactive waste management system at the NuScale Power Plant, noting that liquid, gaseous, and solid waste systems, as well as radiation monitoring, are detailed in Chapter 11. It also outlines the general arrangement of major structures, focusing on the Reactor Building (RXB), which is a reinforced concrete, Seismic Category I structure designed for safety and radiation protection, housing the nuclear power modules (NPMs) in a central reactor pool.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the construction of the pressurizer and its components within the NuScale reactor coolant system, specifying alloys and standards for each part to ensure corrosion resistance and compatibility. It references relevant sections of the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and lists the specific alloys and material specifications for the baffle plate, heater bundles, cover plate, bolting, and heater sheaths.

nuscale_ch01_0051.title: Plant Features of Special Interest
nuscale_ch01_0051.summary: This section describes special plant features of the NuScale Power Plant, highlighting structures such as the Central Utility Building (CUB) and Diesel Generator Buildings (DGB), which house essential but nonsafety-related systems and equipment. It also emphasizes human factors engineering (HFE) considerations, including fail-safe design, automation, digital controls, and workload management to minimize human error and meet regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0014.title: Acronyms and Abbreviations
nuscale_ch01_0014.summary: This section explains the conventions used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) for numbering tables, figures, and pages, as well as the handling of proprietary and sensitive information. Tables and figures are numbered according to their section, and sensitive content is withheld from the public version in accordance with regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0067.title: Drawings and Other Detailed Information
nuscale_ch01_0067.summary: This section describes the inclusion of simplified instrumentation, control, electrical, and mechanical drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information alongside the written text. It references tables and figures listing and explaining the symbols and diagrams used, and notes that site-specific diagrams and legends will be provided by applicants referencing the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch01_0811.title: DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0: Thermal Hydraulic Stability Review ResponsibilitiesII.5 Detect and Suppress Method Trip of reactor before SAFDL violation
nuscale_ch01_0811.summary: The NuScale design does not use the exclusion zone or detect and suppress (D&S) methods for thermal hydraulic stability; instead, existing reactor trip signals act preemptively to prevent violation of safety limits before flow instabilities can develop. The system partially conforms to regulatory criteria by providing more limiting protection than required, but does not actively detect or suppress instabilities, nor does it require backup D&S options.

nuscale_ch01_0314.title: SRP 9.1.5, Rev 1: Overhead Heavy Load Handling SystemsII.4 Specific Criteria to Meet GDC 5
nuscale_ch01_0314.summary: This section summarizes NuScale's conformance with various NRC regulatory criteria for heavy and light load handling systems, specifically addressing compliance with General Design Criteria (GDC) such as GDC 5. The document indicates that NuScale's design meets the specified criteria for overhead heavy load handling systems, with no exceptions noted. Additionally, it clarifies which acceptance criteria apply to design certification versus combined license applicants.

nuscale_ch01_0814.title: DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0: Thermal Hydraulic Stability Review ResponsibilitiesII.8 Detect and Suppress system to monitor process variables and systems.
nuscale_ch01_0814.summary: The NuScale design does not use a Detect and Suppress (D&S) system to monitor process variables for thermal hydraulic stability, as reactor trip signals are designed to act before unstable flow oscillations can develop. As a result, the D&S system is deemed not applicable, since protective actions occur preemptively and stabilities are not detected or suppressed by monitoring systems.

nuscale_ch01_0373.title: SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3: Control Room Area Ventilation SystemII.5 Control of Releases of Radioactive Material to the Environment
nuscale_ch01_0373.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with SRP 9.4.1, Rev 3 requirements for the Control Room Area Ventilation System, specifically regarding the control of radioactive material releases to the environment. It states that NuScale meets the acceptance criteria, except for aspects related to ESF atmosphere cleanup systems, as the control room habitability system uses compressed air tanks for clean air instead of emergency filtration during accident conditions.

edges.description: Section A provides a general overview of the types of drawings included (such as I&C, electrical, or mechanical), while Section B specifically focuses on the electrical and instrumentation and control drawings referenced in Section A.

Section A provides detailed drawings, including piping and instrumentation diagrams, that visually clarify the physical arrangement relevant to the DHRS described in Section B, which outlines the design basis and functional requirements for decay heat removal.

Section A provides detailed drawings and diagrams that specify the physical and functional arrangement of systems described in the general terms of Section B's overall plant description, including the reactor pool and DHRS.

Section A provides detailed diagrams that visually clarify the reactor pool and DHRS component arrangements, which are necessary to assess whether the physical configuration described in Section B (each DHRS train's condenser immersed in the reactor pool for multi-module operation) is adequately supported by the site layout.

Section A provides drawings and figures, such as site layout and system schematics, that visually clarify the arrangement of components relevant to the DHRS described in Section B, which specifies the design basis and operational requirements for decay heat removal.

Section A provides detailed drawings and figures that visually clarify the systems and components generally described in Section B's summary of the plant design, including instrumentation, controls, and safety features relevant to the DHRS and reactor pool arrangement.

Section A provides visual and schematic details (including component positions) that clarify the physical arrangement of systems like the DHRS, which supports the general description in Section B regarding the DHRS trains and their integration with the reactor pool.

Section A specifies the detailed design and function of the DHRS, which is one of the engineered safety features generally described in Section B's summary of plant systems.

Section A describes the DHRS design basis and its function to remove decay heat, while Section B specifies that each DHRS train uses a passive condenser immersed in the reactor pool, directly connecting the DHRS operation to the reactor pool arrangement.

Section A provides a general overview of the plant layout and systems, which includes the reactor pool arrangement referenced in Section B's detailed description of the DHRS equipment and its reliance on the reactor pool for heat removal.

Section A provides a general overview of the plant layout and systems, while Section B specifies the design and function of the DHRS equipment within that layout, detailing how each train interacts with the reactor pool described in Section A.

Section A specifies inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, providing detailed implementation for the general DHRS design basis described in Section B.

Section A provides general regulatory and design requirements for the containment vessel, while Section B offers detailed drawings and diagrams that visually clarify and specify the implementation of those requirements.

Section A describes the containment vessel's design and regulatory compliance, which provides the physical and safety context for the reactor pool arrangement that Section B's DHRS relies on to remove decay heat during multi-module operation.

Section A describes the general arrangement and layout of major plant structures, which provides the spatial context necessary to interpret the detailed electrical and I&C drawings referenced in Section B.

Section A provides the general description and design bases of the plant, while Section B offers detailed drawings (such as P&IDs) that visually clarify and specify the design information referenced in Section A, including arrangements relevant to DHRS operation.

Section A provides the general description and regulatory context for the plant design, while Section B specifies that figures (such as site layout and DHRS diagrams) provide detailed visual information used to clarify and support the design descriptions in Section A.

Section A provides the general plant layout and site arrangement, which establishes the physical context necessary to assess whether the reactor pool configuration described in Figure 1.2-1 can support the multi-module DHRS operation requirements specified in Section B.

Section A references Figure 1.2-2 for the general plant layout, which is analogous to Figure 1.2-1 (site layout) relevant to the analysis question, while Section B describes the use of detailed drawings (such as P&IDs) to clarify system arrangements like the DHRS, thus both sections provide complementary visual and descriptive information necessary to assess the adequacy of the reactor pool arrangement for multi-module DHRS operation.

Section A references the general plant layout (including Figure 1.2-2), while Section B explains that figures (such as those in Section A) provide visual clarification of design information, indicating that Section B supports the use and interpretation of layout figures like those in Section A.

Section A references the general plant layout (including the reactor pool arrangement) in Figure 1.2-2, which provides spatial context for evaluating whether the DHRS described in Section B can function adequately for multi-module operation.

Section A references specific component materials and locations, while Section B provides drawings that visually clarify the arrangement and interconnections of these components, allowing cross-verification of design details.

Section A references specific component materials and their documentation locations, while Section B explains that figures (including drawings) provide visual clarification of such design information, indicating that the figures in Section B may visually represent the components described in Section A.

Section A describes the physical components and materials of the reactor pressure vessel and pressurizer, while Section B outlines the functional requirements and design basis of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS); the connection is that the adequacy of the reactor pool arrangement for multi-module DHRS operation depends on whether the described components in Section A support the DHRS functions specified in Section B.

Section A specifies inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, which are part of the reactor system housed within the containment vessel described in Section B, thus Section A provides details for the general containment context given in Section B.

Section A specifies inspection and classification requirements for DHRS components, which are part of the systems generally described in Section B's overview of the plant design and safety analysis.

Section A describes the physical plant features and utility support structures, while Section B provides the location and purpose of electrical and I&C drawings that visually clarify the design and arrangement of these features, connecting the textual plant description to graphical representations.

Section A (site layout and plant features) provides the physical arrangement and available spaces that must accommodate the DHRS components described in Section B, which specifies the design and operational requirements for multi-module decay heat removal.

Section B explains the numbering and identification system for figures such as Figure 1.2-1 and Figure 5.4-7, which are referenced in the analysis question and may be found in Section A or related sections.

Section A and Section B both provide references to the same set of drawings and figures, with Section B specifically focusing on piping and instrumentation diagrams that are part of the detailed information described in Section A.

Section A provides the site layout and detailed drawings, including the reactor pool arrangement, which are necessary to assess whether the physical configuration supports the multi-module DHRS operation described in Section B's design basis.

Section A outlines the regulatory requirement for reactor trip signals to prevent SAFDL violations, while Section B provides the detailed P&ID drawings that visually clarify the design and arrangement of instrumentation and controls relevant to implementing those trip signals.

Section A outlines the thermal hydraulic stability review responsibilities, including the detection and suppression of instabilities to prevent SAFDL violations, while Section B provides the detailed instrumentation and control drawings that visually clarify how such detection and suppression systems are implemented in the design.

Section A discusses the requirement to detect and suppress thermal hydraulic instabilities to prevent SAFDL violations, while Section B describes the DHRS's design basis to remove decay heat and maintain safe shutdown, both connecting through the need to ensure reactor safety by preventing fuel design limit violations during DHRS operation.

Section A outlines the regulatory criteria and acceptance requirements for systems like the DHRS and spent fuel pool, while Section B provides the detailed drawings and diagrams (including Figures 1.2-1 and 5.4-7) that visually represent the design features referenced in Section A.

Section A outlines the regulatory criteria and conformance requirements for systems like the DHRS and spent fuel pool, while Section B provides the detailed drawings and figures (such as Figure 1.2-1 and Figure 5.4-7) that visually clarify the design's compliance with those criteria.

Section A outlines regulatory criteria and conformance requirements for pool and load handling systems, which set the prerequisites and acceptance criteria that the reactor pool arrangement (as depicted in Figure 1.2-1) must meet to ensure the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) in Section B can function adequately for multi-module operation.

Section A outlines the regulatory requirements and methods for monitoring and suppressing thermal hydraulic instabilities, while Section B provides the detailed drawings and diagrams (including those referenced in the analysis question) that visually clarify the design's instrumentation and control relevant to those requirements.

Section A outlines the regulatory requirements and methods for monitoring and suppressing thermal hydraulic instabilities, while Section B provides the detailed instrumentation and control drawings that visually clarify how these monitoring systems are implemented in the design, thus Section B supports the implementation described in Section A.

Section A discusses the detection and suppression of thermal hydraulic instabilities, ensuring protective actions occur before instability develops, while Section B describes the DHRS's design basis to remove decay heat and maintain safe shutdown, both connecting through the requirement that the reactor pool and DHRS arrangement must prevent instability and maintain safety during multi-module operation.

Section A outlines regulatory requirements and conformance criteria for systems such as ventilation and containment evacuation, while Section B provides the detailed piping and instrumentation diagrams that visually clarify and specify the design implementations referenced in Section A.

Section A outlines regulatory criteria and conformance for systems relevant to reactor safety and environmental protection, while Section B provides the detailed drawings (including those referenced in the analysis question) that visually clarify the design's compliance with those criteria.

Section B describes the design basis and function of the DHRS, which must be evaluated in the context of Section A's regulatory criteria for system sharing and environmental effects to determine if the reactor pool arrangement (from Figure 1.2-1) is adequate for multi-module DHRS operation.

Section A provides detailed design drawings, such as the site layout and DHRS arrangement, which are necessary to assess whether the physical configuration supports the regulatory requirements for DHRS operation as outlined in Section B.

Section A discusses the thermal hydraulic stability and reactor trip mechanisms to prevent SAFDL violations, while Section B outlines criteria for spent fuel pool cooling and handling systems, both of which are connected through their roles in ensuring the adequacy and safety of the reactor pool arrangement for multi-module DHRS operation.

Section A describes the requirement to trip the reactor before SAFDL violation as part of the thermal hydraulic stability review, while Section B specifies the need for a system to monitor process variables and systems to support this detection and suppression function, thus Section B provides details on how the requirement in Section A is operationalized.

Section A outlines the regulatory criteria and conformance for reactor pool and spent fuel pool systems, while Section B details the requirements for monitoring and suppressing thermal hydraulic instabilities, both of which are connected through their relevance to ensuring the adequacy and safety of multi-module DHRS operation as depicted in the site layout and system figures.
